questions.question: What was the Stonewall Uprising (1969), and why is it considered a turning point in LGBTQ+ history?
options: A civil rights march in Washington D.C. demanding equal pay for gay workers
A series of riots in response to police raids at the Stonewall Inn in New York City — a bar serving LGBTQ+ patrons — that sparked the modern gay rights movement
A Supreme Court case that legalized same-sex relationships in New York State
A protest outside the Democratic National Convention demanding inclusion of gay rights in the party platform
questions.answer: A series of riots in response to police raids at the Stonewall Inn in New York City — a bar serving LGBTQ+ patrons — that sparked the modern gay rights movement
questions.explanation: On June 28, 1969, New York police raided the Stonewall Inn (a Mafia-owned bar in Greenwich Village). Raids on gay bars were routine — but this night, patrons fought back for the first time, rioting for several nights. Within months, the Gay Liberation Front and Gay Activists Alliance formed. Within a year, the first Pride marches commemorated the riots' anniversary. Marsha P. Johnson and Sylvia Rivera (transgender activists and frequent Stonewall patrons) were key figures. The event catalyzed organized LGBTQ+ rights advocacy in the US and internationally.
questions.memory_hook: Before Stonewall, simply being gay in a bar was grounds for arrest in New York. The night police raided Stonewall for the last time, the patrons fought back — and the modern LGBTQ+ rights movement began. Pride marches commemorate that night; the word 'pride' was chosen as a deliberate contrast to the shame policing had tried to enforce.

questions.question: The Suffragette movement in Britain was distinct from the American women's suffrage movement. What tactics did British suffragettes famously employ?
options: Exclusively peaceful marches and petition drives to Parliament
Militant civil disobedience: chaining themselves to railings, arson of mailboxes, smashing windows, and hunger strikes in prison — leading to forced feeding by authorities
Legal challenges through the courts, successfully winning the vote for women in 1906
Parliamentary lobbying by allied male politicians who passed the vote for women in 1903
questions.answer: Militant civil disobedience: chaining themselves to railings, arson of mailboxes, smashing windows, and hunger strikes in prison — leading to forced feeding by authorities
questions.explanation: The Women's Social and Political Union (WSPU), founded by Emmeline Pankhurst in 1903, adopted militant tactics after peaceful campaigning failed. Their motto: 'Deeds, Not Words.' Tactics included smashing shop windows, setting fire to letterboxes, and arson attacks on unoccupied buildings. When imprisoned, suffragettes went on hunger strikes and were force-fed — generating international outrage. Emily Wilding Davison died after stepping in front of the King's horse at the 1913 Derby. Women over 30 got the vote in 1918 (as a WWI reward for women's contributions); all women over 21 gained it in 1928.
questions.memory_hook: Emmeline Pankhurst's suffragettes escalated from petitions to property destruction to self-starvation in prison. Force-feeding hunger strikers created horrifying images that turned public opinion. Emily Davison stepped in front of the King's horse at full gallop. The message: we will keep escalating until you cannot look away.

questions.question: The Marshall Islands were the site of extensive US nuclear testing between 1946 and 1958. What test occurred at Bikini Atoll in 1954?
options: The first successful hydrogen bomb test, code-named 'Trinity'

Operation Castle Bravo — the largest US nuclear test (15 megatons, 1,000× Hiroshima), which contaminated nearby islands and the Japanese fishing boat Lucky Dragon, accelerating the international anti-nuclear movement
The first nuclear test conducted in peacetime, proving that nuclear weapons could be safely confined to remote locations
A test designed to demonstrate the safety of nuclear weapons through controlled explosion
questions.answer: Operation Castle Bravo — the largest US nuclear test (15 megatons, 1,000× Hiroshima), which contaminated nearby islands and the Japanese fishing boat Lucky Dragon, accelerating the international anti-nuclear movement
questions.explanation: Castle Bravo (March 1, 1954) was 15 megatons — 2.5× its predicted yield due to an unexpected lithium-6 reaction. It contaminated Bikini Atoll, nearby Marshall Islands (including Rongelap, whose residents suffered radiation sickness), and the Japanese fishing vessel Lucky Dragon No. 5 (crew died of radiation exposure). The Lucky Dragon incident created massive anti-nuclear sentiment in Japan and internationally. The fallout accelerated the Partial Nuclear Test Ban Treaty (1963), which banned atmospheric nuclear tests. Bikini Atoll residents have never been able to permanently return — contamination persists.
questions.memory_hook: Castle Bravo was 2.5 times bigger than predicted because scientists miscalculated the lithium reaction. The fallout poisoned a Japanese fishing boat, killed crew members, and reached Tokyo's fish markets. Japan — which had survived Hiroshima and Nagasaki — was again contaminated by US nuclear tests. The Lucky Dragon incident galvanized the global anti-nuclear movement.

questions.question: Simone Biles is considered the greatest gymnast in history. What makes her floor routine's 'Biles' move distinct?
options: The Biles is a standard back layout with extra height — remarkable only for the consistency with which she performs it

The Biles is a double layout with a half twist — she performs skills that have been named after her that other gymnasts cannot safely execute. She has multiple gymnastics skills named after her, including two on floor and one on vault and balance beam
The Biles refers to her signature dismount off the balance beam, a triple full
The Biles is named for her coach, not herself
questions.answer: The Biles is a double layout with a half twist — she performs skills that have been named after her that other gymnasts cannot safely execute. She has multiple gymnastics skills named after her, including two on floor and one on vault and balance beam
questions.explanation: Simone Biles has had 4 gymnastics skills named after her (as of 2024): two on floor exercise, one on vault, one on balance beam. For a skill to be named after an athlete in competition, they must successfully perform it at a World Championship or Olympics. The Biles (floor) is a double layout with a half-turn in the second salto. Many of her skills were initially not rated highly enough by the Code of Points — critics argued the scoring system discouraged dangerous/innovative skills rather than rewarding them. Biles publicly challenged these ratings. She withdrew from the 2021 Tokyo team final for mental health reasons, then returned to win bronze on beam.
questions.memory_hook: Simone Biles performs moves so difficult that the gymnastics scoring committee initially refused to rate them at their actual difficulty — apparently to discourage others from attempting them. She has four skills named after her, each requiring skills other gymnasts cannot safely execute. She's essentially competing against herself.

questions.question: The 1980 US Olympic hockey team's 'Miracle on Ice' — defeating the Soviet Union 4-3 — is considered the greatest upset in American sports history. What made the Soviet team so dominant before the match?
options: The Soviet team had won every Olympic gold medal since 1956 with a single exception — they had lost to Czechoslovakia in 1976 — and featured players who had been training together as a unit for over a decade under the same coaching system

The Soviet players were officially classified as 'amateurs' under Olympic rules but were in fact professional soldiers paid by the state — a loophole that allowed them to practice full-time while American players had to maintain civilian jobs

The Soviet team had beaten the NHL All-Stars in a 1979 exhibition series 5-1, then demolished the US college squad 10-3 just days before the Olympic tournament — making the final game's outcome completely implausible on paper

The Soviet team's dominance rested on a single innovation: the passing-first offensive system developed by coach Anatoli Tarasov, which produced coordinated attacks no other national team had developed a defensive system to counter
questions.answer: The Soviet team had beaten the NHL All-Stars in a 1979 exhibition series 5-1, then demolished the US college squad 10-3 just days before the Olympic tournament — making the final game's outcome completely implausible on paper
questions.explanation: The 1979 Challenge Cup saw the Soviets beat the NHL All-Stars 6-0 in the deciding game. In a pre-Olympic exhibition just before Lake Placid, they beat the US team 10-3. The Soviet roster included Vladislav Tretiak — widely considered the greatest goaltender in history — and had won four consecutive Olympic golds (1964, 1968, 1972, 1976). The US team was composed entirely of college players, average age 21.5. Herb Brooks's innovative neutral-zone trap system and conditioning program made the difference. The US team then had to beat Finland to win gold — the Soviet game was the semifinal, not the final.
questions.memory_hook: Days before the Olympic tournament, the Soviets beat the US college squad 10-3. They'd beaten the NHL All-Stars 6-0 the previous year. Their roster included Tretiak, the greatest goalie ever. The US team averaged 21.5 years old. And the Soviet game wasn't even the gold medal final — the US still had to beat Finland. They won. Brooks barely spoke to his players afterward.

questions.question: Which 2000 film starring Russell Crowe follows a Roman general enslaved and forced to fight as a gladiator?
options: Troy
Gladiator
Ben-Hur
questions.explanation: Gladiator (2000), directed by Ridley Scott, stars Russell Crowe as Maximus, a Roman general betrayed after Marcus Aurelius's death, enslaved, and turned gladiator seeking revenge against the usurper emperor Commodus (Joaquin Phoenix). The film won 5 Academy Awards including Best Picture and Best Actor (Crowe). It revived the 'sword and sandal' epic genre. The historical Commodus was indeed a real Roman emperor who actually fought as a gladiator, though most of the plot is fictional. Maximus is a fictional character.
questions.memory_hook: Gladiator revived the dead genre of Roman epic films — nothing comparable had been made since the 1960s. The real Commodus was a genuine curiosity: an emperor who performed as a gladiator (with fixed fights) in the arena. Ridley Scott used that historical detail as a plot engine, framing it as the collision between a general who earned his honor and an emperor who bought his.

questions.question: Which author wrote the dystopian novel '1984' and what year was it actually published?
options: Aldous Huxley, published in 1949
George Orwell, published in 1949
Ray Bradbury, published in 1953
H.G. Wells, published in 1945
questions.explanation: George Orwell's Nineteen Eighty-Four (1984) was published on June 8, 1949. Orwell chose the year by inverting the last two digits of 1948 (the year he finished writing it, dying of tuberculosis in 1950 before seeing its success). The novel introduced terms now embedded in culture: Big Brother, doublethink, thoughtcrime, Newspeak, Room 101, and the 'memory hole.' Orwell drew from his experiences in the Spanish Civil War and with Soviet communism. The novel depicts a totalitarian surveillance state in Oceania, where history is continuously rewritten.
questions.memory_hook: Orwell chose 1984 by flipping the last two digits of 1948 — the year he finished writing it. He died the following year of tuberculosis, never knowing 1984 would become one of the most influential novels in history. He named Big Brother after a common British authority figure of the era. The surveillance state he imagined in 1949 looks increasingly prophetic.

questions.question: Diego Velázquez's 'Las Meninas' (1656) has been called the most analyzed painting in Western art history. What makes it so philosophically complex?
options: 'Las Meninas' is unique because Velázquez painted himself into the scene appearing to paint the viewer — while the primary subjects (the king and queen) are visible only as reflections in a background mirror, making the viewer the painting's true subject

'Las Meninas' was the first painting in history to use a vanishing-point perspective system, making it technically groundbreaking — Velázquez invented a mathematical perspective grid that later artists copied but rarely credit

'Las Meninas' contains a hidden self-portrait of Velázquez painting the Infanta — which was considered scandalous because court painters were expected to remain invisible in works depicting royalty — Philip IV reportedly nearly ordered its destruction

'Las Meninas' appears to show a domestic scene but is actually a coded political allegory about the succession crisis following Philip IV's death — art historians have decoded the positions and eye-lines of each figure as representing different noble factions
questions.answer: 'Las Meninas' is unique because Velázquez painted himself into the scene appearing to paint the viewer — while the primary subjects (the king and queen) are visible only as reflections in a background mirror, making the viewer the painting's true subject
questions.explanation: In 'Las Meninas,' Velázquez stands at his canvas on the left, looking outward. The young Infanta Margarita and her ladies-in-waiting occupy the foreground. In a mirror on the back wall, the king and queen (Philip IV and Mariana) are reflected — suggesting Velázquez is actually painting them (and therefore us, the viewers, are where they stand). Michel Foucault devoted the opening chapter of 'The Order of Things' (1966) to analyzing how the painting destabilizes the relationship between viewer, painter, and subject. Picasso painted 58 interpretations of it. It anticipates questions about representation and perspective that dominated 20th-century philosophy of art.
questions.memory_hook: In Las Meninas, the king and queen appear only as a reflection in a mirror — meaning the viewer stands where they stood, making you the subject of the painting you're looking at. Michel Foucault opened his most famous book analyzing it. Picasso painted 58 versions of it. Velázquez stands at the canvas, looking at you, painting you. It's a 400-year-old philosophical trap.

questions.question: The Mississippi River drains water from 31 US states. Into which body of water does it empty?
options: The Atlantic Ocean, near Charleston, South Carolina
The Gulf of Mexico, near New Orleans, Louisiana
The Caribbean Sea, via the Yucatan Channel
The Great Lakes, which then flow to the St. Lawrence Seaway
questions.answer: The Gulf of Mexico, near New Orleans, Louisiana
questions.explanation: The Mississippi River system drains approximately 3.2 million km² — about 41% of the contiguous United States. The river flows ~3,730 km from Lake Itasca, Minnesota to the Gulf of Mexico near New Orleans. Its delta (the 'bird's foot delta') extends into the Gulf. The Mississippi-Missouri river system combined is the fourth longest river system in the world. New Orleans sits below sea level, protected by levees — Hurricane Katrina (2005) demonstrated the catastrophic consequences of levee failure when the city flooded.
questions.memory_hook: The Mississippi drains 41% of the continental US — if it rained in Montana, that water could end up in the Gulf of Mexico. New Orleans sits below sea level at the river's mouth, a city that exists because of engineering. When Katrina breached the levees in 2005, the city's below-sea-level geography made recovery extraordinarily slow.

questions.question: Which country has the most natural lakes in the world?
options: Russia
United States
Brazil
Canada
questions.explanation: Canada contains approximately 879,000 lakes larger than 10 km² — roughly 60% of the world's total. This is partly a legacy of the last ice age: glaciers carved depressions that filled with meltwater. The Canadian Shield region is particularly lake-dense. Canada also contains significant freshwater in the Great Lakes (shared with the US). Russia has the second most lakes by count; Finland is famously 'the land of a thousand lakes' but actually has ~188,000 lakes. Canada's per-capita freshwater availability is among the highest in the world.
questions.memory_hook: Canada has 60% of the world's lakes — a direct consequence of glaciers spending 20,000 years grinding depressions into the Canadian Shield. As the ice melted, every depression filled with water. Canada has more freshwater surface area than any other country and more lakes than the rest of the world combined.

questions.question: What are tectonic plates and what is Pangaea?
options: Tectonic plates are volcanic formations in the Pacific Ocean; Pangaea is the name for the first continent to form after Earth cooled

Tectonic plates are the massive rigid segments of Earth's lithosphere (crust + upper mantle) that float on the semi-fluid asthenosphere and move slowly. Pangaea was the supercontinent that existed ~335-175 million years ago when all current continents were joined
Tectonic plates are the layers of rock beneath each continent; Pangaea was a theory disproved in the 1970s
Tectonic plates only exist under oceans; continents are stationary. Pangaea refers to the first ocean, not a continent
questions.answer: Tectonic plates are the massive rigid segments of Earth's lithosphere (crust + upper mantle) that float on the semi-fluid asthenosphere and move slowly. Pangaea was the supercontinent that existed ~335-175 million years ago when all current continents were joined
questions.explanation: Earth has ~15 major tectonic plates moving 2-10 cm/year. At boundaries: convergent (plates collide, forming mountains or trenches), divergent (plates separate, forming rifts or mid-ocean ridges), transform (plates slide past, creating earthquakes — the San Andreas Fault). Alfred Wegener proposed continental drift in 1912 based on fossil matches, coastline shapes, and rock formations across continents — initially mocked, then confirmed in the 1960s via seafloor spreading evidence. Pangaea began breaking apart ~175 Ma: Laurasia (north) and Gondwana (south) first, then into today's continents.
questions.memory_hook: Alfred Wegener proposed that continents drift in 1912 — based partly on noticing that South America and Africa fit together like puzzle pieces. Scientists mocked him for 50 years. Then seafloor spreading was discovered in the 1960s and confirmed every key prediction of his theory. Wegener died on a Greenland expedition in 1930, never vindicated.

questions.question: What is coral bleaching and why does it threaten reef ecosystems?
options: Coral bleaching is a natural seasonal process where corals shed their outer layer; it poses no long-term threat

Coral bleaching occurs when corals expel the symbiotic algae (zooxanthellae) that give them color and nutrition — triggered by thermal stress (water 1-2°C above normal for weeks). Without algae, corals turn white (bleach) and can starve; prolonged bleaching causes death. Reefs support 25% of all ocean species
Coral bleaching is caused by chemical pollution from sunscreen, which dissolves the coral's pigmentation; it is reversible with water treatment
Coral bleaching refers to the natural end-of-life process of aging coral colonies, which turn white before dying at around 100 years old
questions.answer: Coral bleaching occurs when corals expel the symbiotic algae (zooxanthellae) that give them color and nutrition — triggered by thermal stress (water 1-2°C above normal for weeks). Without algae, corals turn white (bleach) and can starve; prolonged bleaching causes death. Reefs support 25% of all ocean species
questions.explanation: Corals have a mutualistic relationship with zooxanthellae: the algae perform photosynthesis, providing up to 90% of the coral's energy; the coral provides shelter. When water temperatures exceed the coral's threshold, the relationship breaks down and algae are expelled. Reefs can recover from bleaching if temperatures normalize, but repeated events (now annual in some areas due to climate change) prevent recovery. The Great Barrier Reef experienced mass bleaching events in 2016, 2017, 2020, 2022 — consecutive bleaching with insufficient recovery time.
questions.memory_hook: Coral reefs cover less than 1% of the ocean floor but support 25% of all marine species. The coral-algae relationship is a mutualism: the algae make food; the coral provides home. One degree of extra heat breaks the partnership. The Great Barrier Reef has bleached every 2 years since 2016 — not enough time to recover between events.
